nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth sandy and rubble bottoms with moderate water movement; often in turbid or shaded conditions where it anchors via root-like tendrils into fine sand substrate

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighbors

diet.notes: Strictly non-photosynthetic (azooxanthellate) — contains no symbiotic zooxanthellae and cannot derive energy from light. Requires daily target feeding of phytoplankton, rotifers, freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, and fine zooplankton suspensions. At least once-daily feeding is generally recommended; twice daily tends to yield better long-term results. Starvation is considered the leading cause of death in captivity. Supplementation with iodine, strontium, and trace elements is commonly advised.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS and soft corals with spacing
avoidWith: aggressive or large polyp corals placed in direct contact
species that produce strong allelopathic chemicals
heavy-feeding fish that may compete for food particles

summary: Studeriotes longiramosa is an expert-level NPS soft coral with a conifer-tree-like branching form. Azooxanthellate — requires daily phytoplankton feeding and high non-laminar flow; no light requirement.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT SPECIES DISTINCTION: Studeriotes longiramosa (the Christmas Tree Coral) is a stand-alone NPS soft coral (family Paralcyoniidae, order Malacalcyonacea) and is completely unrelated to the product commonly sold as 'Christmas Tree Worm Rock' or 'Porites Christmas Tree Rock' — which is a Porites coral host colonized by Spirobranchus giganteus tube worms (phylum Annelida). These are two entirely different organisms sharing only a common trade name; their care requirements are very different.

Studeriotes longiramosa husbandry notes:

• Non-photosynthetic: This coral contains no zooxanthellae and derives no energy from light. Lighting is generally considered unimportant for its nutrition, though shaded placement is typically advised to reduce algae competition and thermal stress. A PAR range of roughly 20–80 is appropriate — low light that keeps the animal comfortable without promoting nuisance algae. The coral is sometimes described under the informal group 'NPS' (non-photosynthetic) or 'NFSB' (non-feeding symbiotic bacteria) corals.

• Unique morphology and behavior: Unlike most soft corals, Studeriotes does not attach to rock; instead it anchors itself in fine sand or rubble substrate via root-like basal tendrils. It requires at least 5 cm of fine sand to settle and stabilize. It has the remarkable ability to retract its entire trunk and branches into its base (earning it the alternate name 'sphincter coral') — collapsing into a round or oblong potato-like mass at night or when disturbed, then re-expanding into its characteristic conifer-tree form during the day.

• Feeding: Starvation is widely considered the primary cause of death in captivity. Daily feeding of phytoplankton, rotifers, and brine shrimp nauplii is considered the minimum; twice-daily feeding has been associated with better long-term survival. Target feeding directly at the polyps with a pipette or turkey baster is recommended to ensure food reaches the coral before being swept away or consumed by fish.

• Flow: Moderate to strong water movement is generally recommended, with some sources noting that non-laminar (tumbling or gyre-type) flow is preferable to direct laminar jets, which can damage the delicate branches. Strong flow helps deliver food particles to the polyps consistently.

• Water quality: Stable NSW-range chemistry is expected. Supplementation with iodine, strontium, and other trace elements is commonly cited. The species is reported to inhabit somewhat turbid natural conditions, suggesting it is not as sensitive to dissolved organics as ultra-low-nutrient SPS setups.

• Tank suitability: Generally considered an advanced to expert-only species due to its demanding feeding requirements. It has been reported to do relatively well in ecologically rich reef systems with high microbial and planktonic activity, such as mature tanks with live rock and refugiums generating a steady supply of microscopic food particles.

• Colony size: Adult colonies are generally reported to reach 25–30 cm in height. Trade specimens are usually sold at smaller sizes (3–18 cm).

Temperature cross-source note: LiveAquaria states 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); Reeflex (German NPS specialist source) gives 24–29°C. The averaged range of 22–28°C is used here; the lower-end values from LiveAquaria are consistent with a species from Indo-Pacific reef regions.

additional.name: Reeflex – Studeriotes longiramosa (German NPS specialist database)
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–29°C vs LiveAquaria 22–25.6°C — averaged to 22–28°C), adult size (25–30 cm), tank minimum (100L), and expert-only care rating. Also confirmed NPS/azooxanthellate status and fine-sand anchoring requirement.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – O Christmas Tree Coral, how lovely are thy branches
additional.notes: Confirmed NPS status, sand-anchoring behavior, moderate flow recommendation, and trade name (genus Studeriotes, not Sphaerella). Source notes that specimens can fare well in ecology-rich reef aquariums with regular fine-food feeding.

additional.name: WoRMS – Studeriotes longiramosa (authoritative taxonomy)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: confirmed Family Paralcyoniidae, Order Malacalcyonacea, Class Octocorallia. Some older sources list family as Nephtheidae or Viguieriotidae — WoRMS classification adopted as authoritative.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Studeriotes longiramosa
additional.notes: Stub article; confirmed Family Paralcyoniidae, Order Malacalcyonacea. Not used for husbandry parameters.
